Preface

This is the third edition of CSA C22.2 No. 286, Industrial control panels and assemblies, one of a series of
Standards issued by CSA Group under Part II of the Canadian Electrical Code. It supersedes the previous
editions published in 2017 and 2015.

The major changes to this edition include
a)    expansion of requirements for connection means;
b)    additional requirements for ferrules and pigtail adapters;
c)    clarifications for requirements for Class 2 (non-safety control devices) components;
d)    additional compliance criteria for panels and assemblies for use outside of the assumed 0–40° C
      ambient range;

e)    expansion of the short-circuit current rating (SCCR) determination criteria;
f)    revisions to required SCCR markings;
g)    removal of motor control centre requirements; and
h)    addition of strain relief and abnormal operations tests.

In addition, numerous edits have been performed throughout the document to provide additional
information and clarify certain requirements.

For general information on the Standards of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part II, see the Preface of
CSA C22.2 No. 0, General Requirements — Canadian Electrical Code, Part II.

This Standard is considered suitable for use for conformity assessment within the stated scope of the
Standard.

This Standard was prepared by the Integrated Committee on Industrial Control under the jurisdiction of
the Technical Committee on Industrial Products and the Strategic Steering Committee on Requirements
for Electrical Safety, and has been formally approved by the Technical Committee.

This Standard has been developed in compliance with Standards Council of Canada requirements for
National Standards of Canada. It has been published as a National Standard of Canada by CSA Group.

Interpretations: The Strategic Steering Committee on Requirements for Electrical Safety has provided
the following direction for the interpretation of standards under its jurisdiction: "The literal text shall be
used in judging compliance of products with the safety requirements of this Standard. When the literal
text cannot be applied to the product, such as for new materials or construction, and when a relevant
committee interpretation has not already been published, CSA's procedures for interpretation shall be
followed to determine the intended safety principle."

Notes:
1)    Use of the singular does not exclude the plural (and vice versa) when the sense allows.
2)    Although the intended primary application of this Standard is stated in its scope, it is important to note that it
      remains the responsibility of the users of the Standard to judge its suitability for their particular purpose.
3)    This Standard was developed by consensus, which is defined by CSA Policy governing standardization — Code

      the Technical Committee list and yet not be in full agreement with all clauses of this Standard.
4)    To submit a request for interpretation of this Standard, please send the following information to

CSA C22.2 No. 286:23                                Industrial control panels and assemblies

CSA C22.2 No. 286:23
Industrial control panels and assemblies

1 Scope

1.1
This Standard applies to industrial control panels and assemblies rated at not more than 1500 V,
intended to be installed and used in non-hazardous locations in accordance with the rules of the
Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.
Notes:
1)    Examples of industrial control panels and assemblies covered by this Standard include motor control panels,
      process control equipment panels, heating panels, programmable control panels, heating and cooling
      equipment control panels, etc.
2)    The term "control" as used throughout this Standard applies to both starters and controllers.
3)    Electrical instruments that can be included as part of control equipment such as meters, are not covered by
      this Standard.

1.2
The industrial control panels and assemblies covered by this Standard are intended for use in an
ambient temperature of 0 to 40 °C. Additional investigation of the equipment will be required when
equipment is to be used in an ambient temperature outside of this range.

1.3
This Standard does not apply to equipment covered by other CSA Standards, such as but not limited to
a)    power supplies;
b)    automatic transfer switches;
c)    elevator equipment;
d)    enclosed switches;
e)    switchgear;
f)    line isolation monitors;
g)    panelboards and custom panelboards;
h)    hazardous location equipment;
i)    hydro-massage equipment;
j)    power factor correction capacitor banks;
k)    fire pump controllers;
l)    assemblies of equipment intended solely for the distribution of power; and
m)    motor control centre units and sections.

1.4
This Standard does not apply to external loads controlled by the industrial control panels and
assemblies.

1.5
In this Standard, "shall" is used to express a requirement, i.e., a provision that the user is obliged to
satisfy in order to comply with the Standard; "should" is used to express a recommendation or that

3 Definitions

The following definitions shall apply in this Standard:

Accepted component — a component that is not approved to the applicable requirements but that has
been determined to be suitable for use, based on evaluation to the applicable requirements, with
satisfactory results.

Enclosed-type assembly — an industrial control assembly that is provided with an overall enclosure.

Open-type assembly — an industrial control assembly that can include internal wiring, field wiring
terminals, and components that are mounted on a subpanel or backplane but not provided with an
overall enclosure.

Primary circuit — wiring and components that are conductively connected to the supply circuit.

Primary equipment bond — the bonding means that maintains the non-current-carrying parts of the
equipment at the same equipotential as the supply system ground.

Notes:
1)    The primary equipment bond is typically field wired.
2)    The system ground is a ground created at the service equipment or a system source and any associated
      system grounding conductor.

Secondary circuit — wiring and components connected to a circuit, where power is limited by a
transformer, rectifier, voltage divider, or similar device that derives power from a primary circuit, and

Note: For the purposes of this definition, the short-circuit volt ampere limit is the product of the open-circuit volts

Secondary equipment bond — all other bonds within the equipment that maintain the non-current-
carrying parts of the individual components and devices within the equipment at the same
equipotential as the main bond and that are supplied as part of the final assembly.


4 Construction requirements

4.1 General

4.1.1
General requirements applicable to this Standard are given in the latest edition of CSA C22.2 No. 0.

4.1.2
Devices and components used within industrial control panels and assemblies shall be of the kinds or
types and ratings suitable for the specific purposes for which they are to be employed or shall be
evaluated for use in accordance with the applicable requirements.

4.1.3
Electrical components, such as fuses, circuit breakers, snap switches, and transformers, provided as part
of industrial control panels and assemblies shall be suitable for the application and shall comply with
the product standard covering such components.

4.1.4
An accepted component shall comply with the applicable component standard.
Notes:
1)    Component acceptance should be undertaken by an accredited certification body. A component should be
      accepted for use without additional investigation provided the approval of that component has been issued
      by another accredited certification body.
2)    A component or device that has been evaluated under a field evaluation program is not considered to be an
      accepted component without additional review.

4.2 Enclosure

4.2.1 General

4.2.1.1
Clause 4.2 shall only apply to enclosed-type assemblies.

4.2.1.2
The enclosures shall be an approved enclosure or as specified in Clause 4.2.1.3 or 4.2.1.4.

4.2.1.3

An enclosure shall be constructed and assembled in accordance with the requirements as specified in CSA C22.2 No. 94.1 or CSA C22.2 No. 14, or has been previously investigated and documented as suitable for the intended use.

4.2.1.4

Industrial control panels and assemblies supplied with, and located in, walk-in enclosures shall comply with the working space requirements of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.2.2 Openings in enclosures

4.2.2.1

An enclosure, when installed, shall have no openings, other than ventilating openings, that permit the entry of a rod having a diameter greater than 12.7 mm, and any such openings shall comply with Clauses 4.2.2.3 to 4.2.2.5.

4.2.2.2

Ventilating openings in an enclosure, including perforations, louvers, and openings protected by means of wire screening, expanded metal, or a perforated cover, shall be of such size or shape that no opening permits the entry of a rod having a diameter greater than 19 mm for circuits of 600 V or less and 12.7 mm for circuits of 601 to 1500 V. The opening shall also comply with Clauses 4.2.2.3 to 4.2.2.5.

4.2.2.3

The size of the openings described in Clauses 4.2.2.1 and 4.2.2.2, or their location, or both, shall prevent the articulated finger probe, illustrated in Figure 1, from contacting uninsulated live parts. Enamelled wire shall be considered an uninsulated live part.

4.2.2.4

The finger probe specified in Clause 4.2.2.3 shall be applied in any possible configuration, and after insertion through the opening, the configuration shall be changed if necessary.

4.2.2.5

The finger probe specified in Clause 4.2.2.3 shall be used only as a measuring instrument to judge the accessibility provided by the opening. It shall not be used as an instrument to judge the strength of a material. The test finger shall be applied with a force not greater than 11 N.

4.2.2.6

The diameter of the wires of a screen shall be not less than 1.3 mm if the screen openings are 320 mm² or less in area, and shall be not less than 2.06 mm for larger screen openings.

4.2.2.7

Perforated sheet steel and sheet steel employed for expanded metal mesh shall be not less than 1.07 mm thick for mesh openings or perforations 320 mm² or less in area and shall be not less than 2.03 mm thick for larger openings. In a small device, where the indentation of a guard or enclosure will not alter the clearance between uninsulated, movable, live parts and grounded metal so as to adversely

4.2.2.8

Glass covering an observation opening and forming a part of the enclosure shall be reliably secured in
such a manner that it cannot be readily displaced in service and shall provide mechanical protection for
the enclosed parts as follows:
a)    glass covering an opening not more than 100 mm in any dimension shall be not less than 1.4 mm
      thick;
b)    glass covering an opening having no dimension greater than 305 mm shall be not less than
      2.92 mm thick, and
c)    glass used to cover openings larger than permitted by item a) or b) shall be of the clear safety type
      or wire reinforced type and shall comply with the impact test in Clause 7.4.

4.2.2.9

Polymeric transparent materials shall have a minimum flame rating of 5 VA or 5 VB according to
CSA C22.2 No. 0.17, flame test A or flame test B.

4.2.2.10

Electrical equipment shall be so constructed that molten or flaming particles cannot fall to the surface
on or over which the equipment is mounted.

4.2.2.11

Notwithstanding Clause 4.2.2.10, floor-mounted equipment may be marked in accordance with
Clause 5.4.

4.2.3 Enclosure thermal insulation

4.2.3.1

Thermal insulation inside the control panel enclosure shall be retained by mechanical means.

4.2.3.2

In addition to the requirements of Clause 4.2.3.1, adhesive may be used as an additional means for
securing insulation.

4.2.3.3

The spacings between the live parts and the mechanical fastening means or foil-backed insulation shall
not be less than the clearance and creepage distances specified in Table 10 with the thermal insulation
in place.

4.3 Grounding and bonding

4.3.1 General

4.3.1.1

Bonding of industrial control panels and assemblies shall comply with the applicable requirements of
CSA 22.2 No. 0.4.

4.3.1.2

Provision for bonding for industrial control panels and assemblies shall be provided in one of the
following ways:
a)    terminations in enclosures in accordance with Clause 4.3.1.4; or
b)    suitable means to install approved bonding kits.

4.3.1.3

DIN rail-mounted terminations shall be investigated for suitability for bonding termination to the
enclosure.

4.3.1.4

Bonding terminations shall be provided for at least the following number of bonding conductors:
a)    in power device enclosures — one bonding conductor for each incoming and each outgoing power
      circuit, based on one circuit for three poles or less and one circuit for each additional three poles
      or less, plus one bonding conductor for the control circuit, where applicable;
b)    in cast-metal enclosures for power devices that have provision for only one conduit entry — one
      bonding conductor; and
c)    in auxiliary device enclosures — one bonding conductor.

Note: Examples of auxiliary devices include push-button switches and relays.

4.3.1.5

The maximum number of conductors on each termination shall be in accordance with Table 1.

4.3.1.6

For determining the suitability of the bonding termination, the bonding conductor size shall be
a)    No. 14 AWG copper or No. 12 AWG aluminum for control circuits and auxiliary devices; or
b)    in accordance with the requirements of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.3.1.7

Means shall be provided to facilitate grounding of the secondaries of control circuit transformers and
power supply sources.

4.3.1.8

Each different system that is required to be grounded shall have provision for connection to a grounding
conductor.

Where a circuit is required to be grounded, the grounding connection may be made to

have their coatings removed at the point of contact to satisfy with the requirements of Clause 4.3.1.1.

equipment bonds.

4.3.2 Bonding of polymeric enclosures

4.3.2.1
An enclosure employing insulating material, either wholly or in part, shall have a suitable bonding
means to ensure continuity of bonding between all conduit openings and any external metal parts that
can become energized. This suitable bonding means shall be completely assembled to the product or
provided as separate parts for field installation. An enclosure designed for field assembly of the bonding
means shall be provided, with complete instructions to ensure proper installation. The instructions shall
identify the suitable parts and their method of installation (see Clause 5.8).

4.4 Connection means

4.4.1 General

Industrial control panels and assemblies shall be provided with one or more of the supply, load, or supply and load connection means outlined in Clauses 4.4.2 to 4.4.5.

4.4.2 Field wiring terminals and leads

4.4.2.1

Industrial control panels and assemblies, for other than service use, may be provided with wiring terminals or leads for the connection of supply or load conductors having an ampacity not less than the largest of the following:
a)    the ampere rating of the panel or assembly;
b)    125% of the full-load motor-running current specified in Table 2 or 3 for the horsepower rating;
c)    125% of the resistive ampere rating of the devices intended to control fixed electric space-heating equipment loads;
d)    135% of the nominal capacitive current rating of the devices intended to switch capacitors for power factor correction; or
e)    125% of the ampere rating of devices intended for industrial heating controllers.

4.4.2.2

Industrial control panels and assemblies intended for service use shall be provided with wiring leads not smaller than No. 8 AWG or with terminals acceptable for connection of No. 10 AWG or larger conductors.

A lead that is intended to be spliced in the field to a circuit conductor shall be not smaller than No. 18 AWG.

4.4.3 Cord or portable power cable connections

4.4.3.1

When industrial control panels and assemblies intended only for portable or movable applications (such as by forklift or hand-truck) are provided with cord or portable power cable connections to the supply or load, the cord or portable power cable shall be of a size and type of approved hard-usage or extra-hard-usage cord or portable power cable terminating in an appropriate attachment plug or connector.

4.4.3.2

Strain relief shall be provided so that a mechanical stress on a cord or portable power cable will not be transmitted to terminals, splices, or internal wiring. At the point where the cord passes through the enclosure wall, protection shall be provided to prevent abrasion. If such protection is not already approved, the strain relief provided shall meet the test requirements of Clause 7.5.

4.4.3.3

Sizing of cord connections shall be based upon the requirements of Clause 4.4.2 of this Standard and using allowable ampacities of flexible insulated copper conductor cord as per Table of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.4.4.1

When general use, industrial locking or special purpose type receptacles are used only for load
connections, they shall be considered acceptable if they are

When inlet connections, such as cable fittings, cord connectors, or portable power cable type
connectors, are used for supply connections, they shall be approved and intended solely for portable or

Inlets for supply connections shall be only of the male type. Exceptions could apply for neutral and bond
connection points.

Clause 4.4.2.

4.6 Internal wiring

4.6.1

Conductors in an assembly shall be insulated for the highest voltage normally occurring between such
conductors unless
a)    the wires are grouped and provided with a barrier between conductors of different voltage levels;
b)    the wires are positively secured so as to segregate the several voltages from each other per the
      minimum clearance requirements in Table 10; or
c)    circuits involving the wires rated at the lower voltage(s) will not extend beyond the complete
      enclosure.

4.6.2

Insulated conductors shall be suitable for the specific conditions of use intended with respect to
voltage, temperature, and grouping. Conductors shall not be smaller than No. 24 AWG, and the
temperature rating shall be not less than 90 °C unless investigation proves the suitability of other
conductors.

Notes:
1)    The requirements of this Clause apply only to the wiring furnished on or in industrial control equipment as a
      part of the equipment. They do not apply to the supply wiring run to control equipment, to motors, or to
      other apparatus.
2)    For motor and other load applications, the use of Table 7 as a guide in selecting the conductor sizes in
      industrial control panels can obviate the need to perform a temperature test on the conductor.

4.6.3

Conductor sizes for motors, heating loads, transformers, capacitors, etc. shall be in accordance with the
requirements of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.6.4

Notwithstanding Clause 4.6.7, conductors smaller than No. 24 AWG may be used for wiring of printed
circuit boards and interconnecting wiring between electronic modules and sub-assemblies.

4.6.5

Internal wiring shall not be in contact with bare live parts of opposite polarity or with bare live parts of
other circuits.

4.6.6

Wiring shall not be in direct contact with heat generating components, e.g., resistor bodies, heating
elements, heat-sinks, transformer cores, etc.

4.6.7

Individual component standards may provide additional installation requirements, particularly with
reference to the following considerations:
a)    heating effects under normal conditions of use and abnormal conditions that are likely to arise in
      service; and
b)    protection of bare or insulated live parts to prevent contact by persons or objects.

Note: Film-coated wire is considered to be a bare live part.

4.6.8

Wires shall be supported or secured or otherwise run in suitable raceways, in order that they will not
come into contact with moving parts or rest on sharp edges or projections that can cause abrasion of
the insulation. Wires shall be of flexible or extra-flexible construction where they make connection to

Bonding conductors shall be bare or insulated with a continuous outer finish that is either green or

4.6.12 Wiring ferrules and pin (pigtail) adapters

4.6.12.1

Wiring ferrules suitable for use with stranded copper wire(s) may be used. The connector used to
terminate the ferrules shall be suitable for both the wire sizes and number of wires crimped inside. The
pin of the ferrule shall be within the range of wire sizes marked on the wire connector.

4.6.12.2

Wire connector adapters, pin (solid) or pigtail (stranded) type, and associated connectors, suitable for
use with stranded copper wire(s), may be used. The barrel of the adapter shall be suitable for both the
wire size and quantity of wires crimped inside. The wire connector used to terminate the adapter shall
be suitable for use with the size of the adapter pin or pigtail.

4.6.12.3

Crimping shall be in accordance with the ferrule/pin adapter manufacturer's instructions and shall
include the use of the appropriate crimping tool. The number of wires and wire sizes shall not exceed
the ferrule/pin adapter manufacturer's recommendations; the final crimp shall be secure, and there
shall be no stray strands of wire protruding beyond the sleeve of the ferrule/pin adapter.

4.6.12.4

The use of a ferrule or pin adapter shall not result in the reduction of electrical spacings when installed
in the wiring connector.

Note: Stripping the wire insulation back too far on the wire(s) or allowing stray strands of protruding wire might
result in a reduction of spacing.

4.7 Installation of components

4.7.1

Component parts of industrial control panels and assemblies shall be of types specifically evaluated for the use intended or shall be investigated with, and as an integral part of, the equipment.

4.7.2

Electrical components, such as fuses, circuit breakers, snap switches, and transformers, provided as part of industrial control panels and assemblies shall be suitable for the application and shall comply with the applicable standards covering such components.

4.7.3

Devices that are not approved, such as indicating instruments, analog voltmeters and ammeters, and solid-state devices such as silicon controlled rectifiers, might be subject to additional investigation.

4.7.4

Electrical components and special shielded low voltage wires, thermocouple leads, etc., that are not approved are acceptable in Class 2 limited energy circuits provided that they are installed in accordance with the requirements of Section 16 of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.7.5

Provision shall be made for securely mounting components to a supporting surface. A bolt, screw, or other part used to mount a component of the equipment shall not be used for securing the complete device to the supporting surface. Panel-mounted devices that can be handled in normal operation, such as switches and rheostats, shall be staked or otherwise prevented from rotating. Friction shall not be considered an acceptable method of preventing rotation.

4.7.6

Enclosures required to be opened during normal operation, thus exposing bare live parts, shall have
a)    doors hinged such that the door will not come off inadvertently; and
b)    barriers installed to prevent contact with bare live parts during normal operation.

Note: The replacement of fuses is not considered a normal operation with respect to industrial control equipment, but the resetting of overload devices, repeated adjustment of timers or switches, etc., are considered normal operations.

4.7.7

A single-throw knife switch shall be connected to that the blade or blades will be dead when the switch is open unless the switch is designed so that all live parts are inaccessible when the switch is in the open position.

4.7.8

A single throw knife switch shall be mounted so that gravity will not tend to close it.

4.7.9

Branch and feeder disconnects shall comply with the applicable requirements of CSA C22.2 No. 4, CSA C22.2 No. 5, or CSA C22.2 No. 14.

4.8 Protection for industrial control panels and assemblies

4.8.1 General

4.8.1.1

Branch circuit protection shall comply with the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I, Sections 14, 26, and 28,
as applicable.

4.8.1.2

Motor branch circuits shall be protected against both short-circuit and overload by approved fuses,
thermal-magnetic circuit breakers, or combination motor controllers. If protection is not provided with
the panel, then the panel shall be marked according to Clause 5.18.
Note: Some solid-state devices can incorporate overload protection.

4.8.1.3

Manual starters, moulded case switches, tap circuit protectors and supplementary protectors,
supplementary fuses, semi-conductor fuses, etc., shall not be used for branch circuit protection.

4.8.1.4

Overload relays shall be of proper size to cover the correct current range ensuring that an appropriate
heater element can be selected for the intended horsepower rating as detailed in the manufacturer's
instruction and marked in accordance with Clause 5.22.

4.8.1.5

For replaceable thermal overload relay types only, the self-adhesive label indicating the proper current
element selection shall be affixed adjacent to the overload relays.

     motor controller;

d)   protection is provided by other means that comply with the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.8.2.4

If the rated secondary current of a transformer [see Clause 4.8.2.2 b)] is 2 A or more, the current rating
of the secondary overcurrent device may be as specified in Table 14. If the rated primary current of the
transformer is 9 A or more and 125% of this current does not correspond to a standard rating of fuse or
non-adjustable circuit-breaker, the next higher standard rating of protective device shall be used.

4.8.2.5

A control transformer and its primary and secondary conductors may be protected by overcurrent
devices located only in the primary circuit, provided that the
a)   transformer is single phase and has only a two-wire (single-voltage) secondary;
b)   maximum value of an intended overcurrent device is determined in accordance with
     Clause 4.8.2.2; and
c)   maximum value of an intended overcurrent device as determined in accordance with Item b) does
     not exceed the value of an overcurrent device obtained from Table 12 for the secondary conductor
     multiplied by the secondary-to-primary voltage of the transformer.

4.8.2.6

The same overcurrent protective device may be used for both control-circuit transformer protection
and primary-circuit conductor protection where its rating satisfies each requirement.

4.9 Supplementary protection

4.9.1

Supplementary protectors rated for industrial use may be used in any circuit where branch circuit protection is not required or is already provided by another device. Supplementary protectors with a U3 rating and a minimum short-circuit current rating (SCCR) of 5 kA may be used for control circuit protection.
Note: See Annex D.

4.9.2

Notwithstanding Clause 4.9.1, supplementary protectors with Application Codes U1a, C1a, U1, and C1 (or no code) shall not be relied upon to provide either disconnect or isolation functions and shall be marked in a location visible to the operator and in accordance with Clause 5.30.

4.9.3

Supplementary protectors shall not be used for branch circuit protection (see Figure D.5). See also Clause 4.7.2.

4.9.4

Supplementary protectors shall be a type suitable for the function intended.
Note: A supplementary protector could provide under voltage trip, over voltage trip, overload trip, etc.

4.9.5

The short-circuit rating of the supplementary protector shall be adequate for the circuit in which it is used and shall be 2 kA minimum for a control circuit with an external source; or as determined by the VA/[Sec voltage × % impedance (z)] of a single-phase internal control transformer.

4.9.6

The line side overcurrent protection shall be provided in accordance with the manufacturer's literature.

4.9.7

The contact rating of the supplementary protector shall be suitable to interrupt rated overload currents. (See application codes – OL0 and OL1 in Table D.3.)

4.9.8

Where the supplementary protector is required to isolate a load from the source for personnel protection during maintenance, the protector shall have a suitable short-circuit application code rating including U3 or U1a. (See application codes – U3 and U1a in Table D.1.)
Note: A supplementary protector might not satisfy the requirements of CSA Z460 for lockout or CSA Z462 for personnel protection.

4.9.9

Where the supplementary protector is intended to protect a component or circuit from overheating due to overload, the appropriate level of protection shall be provided. (See application codes TC0, TC1, TC2, and TC3 in Table D.2.)

4.10 Disconnecting means

4.10.1

A disconnecting means shall be provided for each incoming supply circuit.

4.10.2

Notwithstanding Clause 4.10.1, a disconnecting means is not required if the panel is marked in
accordance with Clause 5.26.

4.10.3

When the disconnecting means is a circuit breaker, molded case switch, or fused switch, the maximum
continuous load shall not exceed 80% of the device rating except for those devices marked as suitable
for continuous operation at 100% of their ratings.

4.10.4

A disconnecting means for a motor branch circuit shall be sized and rated in accordance with Section 28
of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.

4.10.5

The centre of the grip of an operating handle for the disconnecting means provided in a floor-mounted
controller, when in its highest position, shall not be more than 2 m above the surface on which the
operator is intended to stand.

4.10.6

The construction of an industrial control panel incorporating a fuseholder, and the location of fuses the
normal function of which requires renewal, shall be such that fuses will be readily accessible when the
switch contacts are open, to allow replacement without exposure to any live part. The electrical
arrangement of a single-throw switch shall be such that, if properly connected, fuse terminals are dead
when the switch contacts are open.

Note: A control-circuit fuse is not considered to require renewal as a normal function, provided that the fuse and
control-circuit load (other than a fixed control-circuit load, such as a pilot light) are within the same enclosure.

4.10.7

When two or more sources of supply are employed, Clauses 4.10.8 to 4.10.10 shall apply.

4.11 Instantaneous trip breakers

4.11.1

An instantaneous-trip circuit breaker shall be part of an approved combination motor starter or controller that also provides overload protection. (See Section 28 of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.)

a) ambient temperature;

c) enclosure material;
d) thermal contribution from other installed components (e.g., transformers, reactors, fuses, etc.);
and
e) temperature ratings of individual components.

Notwithstanding the requirements of Clause 4.12.1, compliance may be determined by conducting the temperature test in Clause 7.2.

4.13 Assembly short-circuit current ratings

4.13.1 Control circuits tapped from feeder circuits

Branch circuit protective devices shall be provided for the overcurrent protection for
a)      the common control circuit;
b)      the primary of a control transformer; or
c)      the primary of a control circuit power supply.

The branch circuit protective devices shall have an interrupt rating not less than the overall panel SCCR.
Note: See Canadian Electrical Code, Part I, Figure B28-1 for additional details for a motor circuit.

4.13.2 Control circuits tapped from the load-side of a motor branch circuit
protective device

4.13.2.1

The SCCR for the overcurrent protection for the following shall be included in the determination of the
branch SCCR as per Clause 4.13.9.1 and 4.13.9.2:
a)      the common control circuit;
b)      the primary of a control transformer; or
c)      the primary of a control circuit power supply.

4.13.2.2

For the purpose of applying Clauses 4.13.1 and 4.13.2.1, any secondary circuit that is supplying a servo
drive and operates at 24 Vdc maximum and is supplied from a source with a maximum output power of
100 VA shall be considered a control circuit.

4.13.3 Determining assembly SCCRs

Notes:
1)      The default method for determining the industrial control panel's overall SCCR is to apply the lowest SCCR or
        interrupting rating of any individual component within the primary circuit of the assembly.
2)      An alternative technique is to determine the short-circuit ratings of the individual power circuit components,
        investigate opportunities to increase the SCCR of individual circuits using current limiting components, and
        then determine the final SCCR for the complete assembly.

4.13.3.1 Individual power circuit component SCCRs

4.13.3.1.1

All power circuit components, for feeder and branch circuits, shall have a SCCR expressed in amperes or
kiloamperes and voltage.

Note: Examples include
a)      disconnect switches;
b)      branch circuit fuses/holders;
c)      branch circuit protective devices;
d)      bus bars;

4.13.5 Power transformers used to limit the available short-circuit current

4.13.5.1

Where feeder and branch circuit components and overcurrent protective devices are supplied by a
power transformer with an isolated secondary winding, the short-circuit current rating on the line side
of the transformer shall be determined by the methods described in Clauses 4.13.5.2 to 4.13.5.5.

4.13.5.2

The available fault current (Iavailable) on the secondary side of a power transformer, with a marked or
known impedance, shall be calculated as specified in Annex C.
Note: The calculated available fault current is line-to-line.

4.13.5.3

Where the impedance of the power transformer is not marked or has a known impedance of not less
than 2.1%, the impedance may be assumed to be 2.1%.

4.13.5.4

Where the short-circuit current rating of all components and interrupting rating of all overcurrent
protective devices supplied by the transformer are greater than or equal to the calculated available
fault current (Iavailable), the short-circuit current rating on the line side of the power transformer circuit
may be the interrupting rating of the primary overcurrent protective device.

4.13.5.5

Where the SCCR of one or more of the components or the interrupting rating of one or more of the
overcurrent protective devices supplied by the transformer are less than the calculated available fault
current (Iavailable), the SCCR on the line side of the power transformer circuit shall be the lowest of either
a)   the lowest SCCR of the components; or
b)   the lowest interrupting rating of the overcurrent protective devices supplied by the transformer.

4.13.6 Current limiting circuit breakers used to limit the available short-circuit
current in feeder circuits

4.13.6.1

Where a feeder circuit supplying branch circuits is protected by a circuit breaker marked "current
limiting", the SCCR on the line side of the circuit breaker shall be determined by the methods described
in Clauses 4.13.6.2 to 4.13.6.4.

4.13.6.2

To select a maximum SCCR for the feeder circuit breaker circuit, the following conditions shall be met:
a)   All individual components in the branch circuit shall have a SCCR that is equal to or greater than
     the published peak let-through current corresponding to the specific circuit breaker, ampacity,
     voltage, and selected available short-circuit current.
b)   The interrupting rating of all branch circuit protective devices or the SCCR of any combination of
     motor controller on the load side shall be equal to or greater than the available short-circuit
     current selected for the feeder circuit breaker.

4.13.9 Overall SCCR of the panel

4.13.9.1 Determining each branch circuit short-circuit rating
The SCCR of each branch circuit provided with branch circuit protection is the smallest of the following:
a)    the smallest SCCR of all the power circuit components on the load side of a branch circuit
      protective device;
b)    the overcurrent protection of the control circuits as determined in Clauses 4.13.2 and 4.13.3; and
c)    the short current rating of the branch circuit protective device.

4.13.9.2 Determining the overall short-circuit rating of the panel

4.13.9.2.1
The SCCR of the industrial control panel shall be the lowest SCCR for any power circuit component and
the control circuit overcurrent protection identified in Clause 4.13.3 for a panel that consists of a single
branch circuit and not provided with branch circuit protection within the panel. For panels that contain
only control circuit components, a short-circuit rating is not required.

4.13.9.2.2
The SCCR of the industrial control panel shall be in accordance with Clause 4.13.9.1 for a panel that
consists of a single branch circuit including branch circuit protective devices and power circuit
components within the panel.

4.13.9.2.3
For a panel that consists of multiple branch circuits, and feeder components within the panel (such as
disconnecting switches, bus bars, terminal blocks, and feeder overcurrent protective devices), the SCCR
of the industrial control panel shall be the lowest of
a)    the lowest SCCR of any branch circuit in accordance with Clause 4.13.9.1 that has not been
      modified by Clauses 4.13.5 to 4.13.7;
b)    the SCCR of any feeder component not covered by item c) below and any control circuit
      overcurrent protection connected to the feeder as described in Clause 4.13.2; or
c)    the modified SCCR determined from Clauses 4.13.5 to 4.13.7 for each branch circuit supplied by
      the associated feeder component.

7 Testing

7.1 General

Industrial control panels, when testing is required by Clause 4 of this Standard, shall be investigated by
subjecting a representative sample or samples to the applicable test described in Clause 7. The tests
shall be conducted at rated frequency and at the test voltage specified.

7.2 Temperature

7.2.1

The test specified in Clauses 7.2.2 to 7.2.17 shall be performed as required by Clause 4.12.2 or 4.12.8.

7.2.2

Industrial control panels, when tested under the conditions described in Clauses 7.2.3 to 7.2.17, shall
not attain a temperature at any point high enough to constitute a fire hazard or to affect adversely any
materials employed in the device, nor shall it show temperature rises at specific points greater than
those specified in Table 15.

7.2.3

The temperature test shall be conducted at any ambient temperature within the range of 10 to 40 °C.
The ambient temperature may be determined using thermometers or thermocouples placed as
specified in Clause 7.2.9.

7.2.4

An industrial control panel shall be constructed so that the temperature rise on the back of the device
will not exceed 50 °C above a room temperature of 40 °C (i.e., 90 °C) when operated under normal
conditions, or the construction shall be such that only the points of support will be in contact with a
plane mounting surface, with the remainder of the device spaced at least 6.3 mm from the mounting
surface.

7.2.5

To determine whether an industrial control panel complies with the temperature test requirements, the device shall be operated under normal conditions and shall carry its rated current continuously at the test voltage of the upper limit of the nominal voltage rating of the device until temperatures are constant. An overload relay shall not trip during the test. Any convenient source of supply may be used for temperature tests on parts other than coils.

7.2.6

The tests on all parts shall be made simultaneously because the heating of one part can affect the heating of another part.

7.2.7

An industrial control panel shall be tested at rated current with 1220 mm of copper wire attached to each field-wiring terminal. The wire shall be of the smallest size, having an ampacity of at least 125% of the test current for motor loads and at least 100% for other loads. Wire size shall be determined in accordance with Table 19, based on the wire temperature rating marked on the equipment. The type of insulation is not specified, but the colour shall be black. The temperature test may be conducted with conductors having other than black insulation, but reference temperature measurements shall be conducted with black insulated conductors. The type of insulation is not specified. If the terminal will not receive the size of wire required for testing at rated current, the maximum allowable wire size shall be used.

7.2.8

If reference measurements of ambient temperatures are necessary, several thermometers shall be placed at different points around the equipment at a distance of 900 to 1800 mm. The thermometers shall be located in the path of the cooling medium but protected from draughts and abnormal heat radiation. The ambient temperature shall be the mean of the readings of the temperatures taken at equal intervals of time during the final quarter of the test.

7.2.9

The suitability of insulation materials, other than those covered by Table 15, shall be determined with respect to properties such as flammability, arc resistance, etc., based on an operating temperature equal to 40 °C plus the measured temperature rise.

7.2.10

For a device employing a fuseholder, an unplated copper bar, unplated copper tubing, or an equivalent material with negligible impedance may be used during the test instead of a regular fuse.

7.2.11

The thermocouple method shall consist of the determination of temperature by the application of thermocouples to the hottest accessible parts.

7.2.12

The formula for obtaining the temperature of copper by the rise-of-resistance method shall be as specified in Clause 7.2.3 of CSA C22.2 No. 0.

7.2.13

The preferred method of measuring the temperature of a coil shall be the rise of resistance method,
but temperature measurements by either the thermocouple or resistance method are acceptable. The
thermocouple method shall not be employed for a temperature measurement at any point at which
supplementary insulation is employed.

7.2.14

As it is generally necessary to de-energize the winding of a coil before measuring its resistance, the
value of resistance at shutdown may be determined by taking several resistance measurements at short
intervals, beginning as quickly as possible after initiating shutdown. A curve of the resistance values and
the time shall be plotted and extrapolated to give the value of resistance at shutdown.

7.2.15

Temperatures shall be measured using a potentiometer-type instrument and thermocouples consisting
of wires not larger than No. 30 AWG or, subject to investigation, not larger than No. 24 AWG.

7.2.16

A temperature rise shall be considered to be constant when three successive readings, taken at
intervals of 10% of the previously elapsed duration of the test (but not less than 10 min), are constant
within 1 °C.

7.2.17

A thermocouple junction and adjacent thermocouple lead wire shall be securely held in good thermal
contact with the surface of the material on which the temperature is being measured.
Note: In most cases, adequate thermal contact will result from securely taping or cementing the thermocouple in
place, but if a metal surface is involved, brazing or soldering the thermocouple to the metal can be necessary.

7.3 Dielectric strength
Note: Refer to Annex E for additional information on production line dielectric strength testing.

7.3.1

An industrial control device shall withstand for 1 min without breakdown the application of a 60 Hz
essentially sinusoidal voltage of not less than
a)    500 V for devices rated 50 V or less;
b)    1000 V plus twice the maximum rated voltage for devices rated at 51 to 750 V; and
c)    2000 V plus 2.25 times the rated voltage for devices rated at 751 to 1500 V.

7.3.2

The applicable voltage shall be applied between
a)    bare live parts and the enclosure with the contacts open and closed;
b)    terminals of opposite polarity with the contacts closed; and
c)    bare live parts of different circuits.

7.3.3

A transformer, a coil, an electronic device not used as an isolation device, or a similar device normally
connected between lines of opposite polarity shall be disconnected from one side of the line during the
test specified in Clause 7.3.2 b).

7.3.4

If an industrial control device involves a meter or meters, such instruments may be disconnected from
the circuit and the complete device subjected to a dielectric strength test as specified in Clauses 7.3.1
to 7.3.3. The meter or meters shall then be tested separately for dielectric strength with an applied
voltage of
a)    1000 V in the case of an ammeter;
b)    1000 V plus twice the rated voltage in the case of any other instrument having a potential circuit;
c)    500 V for meters in circuits operated at 50 V or less (no test requirements are specified for circuits
      operated at 30 V or less); and
d)    2000 V ac plus 2.25 times the maximum rated voltage for devices rated above 750 V applied
      between power supply circuits and any non-current-carrying metal parts.

7.3.5

If industrial control equipment contains printed wiring assemblies and other electronic circuit
components that would be affected adversely by application of the test voltage, or that are designed to
protect the equipment from voltage, they shall be removed, disconnected, or otherwise rendered
inoperable before the dielectric tests are made. A representative sub-assembly may be tested instead of
an entire unit. The insulation and spacings of circuits using these devices shall then be tested separately
for dielectric strength, with an applied voltage of not less than
a)    1000 V ac plus twice the maximum rated voltage between the low-voltage power supply circuit and
      any exposed non-current-carrying metal parts;
b)    1000 V ac plus twice the maximum rated voltage between live parts of the low-voltage circuit and
      live parts of circuits operated at 50 V or less;
c)    500 V ac between exposed non-current-carrying metal parts and circuits operated at 50 V or less
      (no test requirements are specified for circuits operated at 30 V or less); and
d)    2000 V ac plus 2.25 times the maximum rated voltage for devices rated above 750 V applied
      between power supply circuits and any non-current-carrying metal parts.

7.3.6

To determine whether industrial control equipment complies with the requirements of Clauses 7.3.1,
7.3.2, 7.3.4, and 7.3.5, the device shall be tested by means of a suitable 500 VA or larger-capacity
transformer, the output voltage of which is essentially sinusoidal and can be varied. The applied voltage
shall be increased from zero to the required value at a substantially uniform rate and as rapidly as is
consistent with its value being correctly indicated by a voltmeter. The voltage shall be held at that value
for 1 min.

7.3.7

Where it is more convenient to do so, the dielectric strength test may be conducted by applying a dc
voltage instead of an ac voltage, provided that the voltage used is 1.4 times the values specified in
Clause 7.3.

7.4 Resistance to impact for observation openings

7.4.1

The test shall be conducted on a single sample at an ambient temperature between 10 and 40 °C.

7.4.2

The test shall be conducted with an impact as follows:
a)   7.0 ± 0.2 J for materials relied upon for protection against shock, fire, or mechanical hazards;
b)   0.5 J for materials for applications other than those covered in item a); or
c)   0.5 J for a polymeric enclosure containing only extra-low voltage components defined as a
     maximum of 30 Vac or 42.2 V peak, and 15 W.

7.4.3

A single impact shall be applied at right angles to the surface with the equipment in its normal position.

7.4.4

The impact shall not cause any of the conditions specified in Clause 7.4.6 to occur.

7.4.5

The impact shall be applied by a solid, smooth steel sphere, 50 ± 1 mm in diameter, weighing
approximately 0.53 kg. For top surfaces, the steel sphere shall be allowed to fall freely from rest
through the distance required to cause it to strike the enclosure when the sphere has the specified
energy. For surfaces other than the top, the steel sphere shall be suspended by a fine wire and allowed
to fall as a pendulum through the distance required to cause it to strike the surface with the specified
impact, and the enclosure shall be placed so that the surface to be tested is vertical and in the same
vertical plane as the point of support of the pendulum.

7.4.6

The impact shall not
a)   reduce spacings below the minimum acceptable values;
b)   make any bare live parts or internal wiring accessible to contact;
c)   have an undue adverse effect on the insulation; and
d)   produce any other condition that might increase the risk of shock, fire, or damage to the
     equipment.

With respect to item d), the equipment shall comply with the dielectric voltage withstand requirements
applicable to the equipment after being subjected to the impact test.

7.5 Strain relief

The means of strain relief provided on a flexible cord shall withstand, without damage to the cord or
conductors and without displacement, a direct pull of 156 N applied to the cord for 1 min. Supply
connections within the equipment shall be disconnected from terminals or splices during the test when
applicable.

7.6 Abnormal operations test

7.6.1

Before all operation tests, the test sample shall be mounted, connected, and operated as described in
the temperature test in Clause 7.2.

7.6.3
The individual tests shall be performed until terminated by
a)    the operation of an internal or external protective device or mechanism;
b)    a component failure; or

7.6.4
As a result of the abnormal operation tests, the equipment shall comply with the following:
a)    There shall be no emission of flame or molten metal.
b)    The surgical cotton indicator shall not have ignited.
c)    No door or cover shall have blown open.
d)    The equipment is not required to be operational after testing, and the enclosure could become
      deformed.

7.6.5
For equipment with a combination of cooling mechanisms (e.g., forced air and other means), all
relevant tests shall be performed. It is not necessary to perform the tests simultaneously.

7.6.6
Equipment with forced ventilation shall be operated at rated load with the blower motor or motors
made inoperative.

7.6.7
Equipment having filtered ventilation openings shall be operated with the openings blocked to
represent clogged filters. The test shall be performed initially with the ventilation openings blocked
50%. The test shall be repeated with the ventilation openings fully blocked.

Annex C (normative)
Calculating available fault current

Note: This Annex is a mandatory part of this Standard.

C.1
The following equations can be used to calculate the available short-circuit current where required by
Clauses 4.13.5.2 and 4.13.11.1:

Iavailable = (VAtrans) / (Vsec * %Z) - for 1 phase transformer

Iavailable = (VAtrans) / (√3 * Vsec * %Z) - for 3 phase transformer

where
Iavailable      =   available fault current on the secondary of the transformer, A
VAtrans         =   VA rating of the transformer, volt-amperes
Vsec            =   secondary voltage of the transformer, V
%Z              =   impedance of the transformer (expressed in the decimal format)

C.2
Some common values of available fault current on the secondary side of single phase transformers are
provided in Tables C.1 to C.4. for reference.

Notes:
1)   Calculated single phase, 2-wire secondary short-circuit currents are line-to-line.
2)   On a single phase, 3-wire secondary (e.g., 120/240V) the line-to-neutral short-circuit is 1.5 times the line-to-
     line short-circuit.
3)   Calculated three-phase, 3-wire short-circuits are line-to-line.
4)   On a three-phase, 4-wire secondary (e.g., 347/600V) the line-to-neutral short-circuit is 1.25 times the line-to-
     line short-circuit.

CSA C22.2 No. 286.23                                Industrial control panels and assemblies

Annex D (informative)
Guidelines for the use of supplementary protectors

Note: This Annex is not a mandatory part of this Standard.

D.1 Supplementary protector
A supplementary protector is a manually resettable device designed to open the circuit automatically on
a predetermined value(s) of time versus current or voltage within electrical equipment and is covered in
CSA 22.2 No. 235. A manual means for opening the circuit may also be provided.

D.2 Usage
Supplementary protectors are not to be used as a substitute for branch circuit overcurrent devices or in
place of branch circuit protective devices specified in Section 14 of the Canadian Electrical Code, Part I.
As there is sometimes confusion on where a supplementary protector is acceptable for use, several
figures have been included here from other sources to illustrate both proper and improper use. Please
refer to CSA 22.2 No. 235 for additional limitations.

D.3 Figures
Figure D.1 provides general application information. Figures D.2, D.3, and D.4 provide other examples of
proper application. Figure D.5 illustrates an improper use.

D.4 Application codes

D.4.1
Supplementary protectors are marked with an application code. The codes meaning is as described in
Clauses D.4.2 to D.4.4. For additional information, refer to CSA 22.2 No. 235.

D.4.2
The tripping current (TC) codes are as follows:
a)   TC0 — tripping current is less than 125% of ampere rating;
b)   TC1 — tripping current is between 125 and 135% of ampere rating;
c)   TC2 — tripping current is more than 135% of ampere rating; and
d)   TC3 — tripping current and time is standardized at 135% and at 200% of ampere rating.

D.4.3
The overload rating (OL) codes designate whether the supplementary protector has been tested for
general use or motor starting applications. An OL code is always associated with a voltage rating (e.g.,
OL0 120 VAC):
a)   OL0 — tested at 1.5 times ampere rating for general use; and
b)   OL1 — tested at 6 times ac rating or 10 times dc rating for motor starting applications.

D.4.4
The C and U codes are used as applicable (see Table D.1). The SCCR (SC) in kiloamperes is followed by a
letter and number designating the test conditions, and any calibration and dielectric strength tests
following the short-circuit test:
a)   C — a short-circuit test was conducted with series overcurrent protection; and
b)   U — a short-circuit test was conducted without series overcurrent protection.

1) Supplementary protectors have been proven to have interrupt capability at values of fault current equal to the short-circuit rating of the protector when applied in single phase circuits or 3-phase circuits where the full phase-to-phase voltage cannot occur across any single pole.
2) Supplementary protectors can isolate the circuit but should not be used as a disconnect.
3) Supplementary protectors have not been proven to isolate the circuits under all conditions.

These lower fault levels might result in the protector attempting to interrupt fault energy for which it was not designed.
5) The isolation of the circuit is due to the short-circuit current causing damage to the internal circuitry of the supplementary protector to the extent that continuity cannot be re-established.
6) Operability of the supplementary protector should be verified if the device trips.

Annex E (informative)
Production line dielectric strength testing

Note: This informative Annex has been written in mandatory language to facilitate adoption by anyone wishing to do so.

E.1
An industrial control panel shall withstand, for 1 min without breakdown, the application of a 60 Hz essentially sinusoidal voltage of not less than
a)    500 V for circuits rated 50 V or less;
b)    1000 V plus twice the maximum rated voltage for circuits rated at 51 to 750 V; and
c)    2000 V plus 2.25 times the rated voltage for circuits rated at 751 to 1500 V.

E.2
As an alternative to the voltage specified in Clause E.1, a potential 20% higher may be applied for one second.

E.3
The applicable voltage shall be applied between live parts and exposed noncurrent-carrying metal parts.

E.4
A transformer, a coil, an electronic device not used as an isolation device, or a similar device normally connected between lines of opposite polarity may be disconnected from one side of the line during the test.

E.5
If an industrial control panel involves a meter or meters, such instruments may be disconnected from the circuit during the test.

E.6
If industrial control panel contains printed wiring assemblies and other electronic circuit components that would be affected adversely by application of the test voltage, or that are designed to protect the equipment from voltage, they may be removed, disconnected, or otherwise rendered inoperable before the dielectric tests are made.

E.7
To determine whether industrial control panel complies with the dielectric strength requirements, the panel shall be tested by means of a suitable 500 V•A or larger-capacity transformer, the output voltage of which is essentially sinusoidal and can be varied. The applied voltage shall be increased from zero to the required value at a substantially uniform rate and as rapidly as is consistent with its value being correctly indicated by a voltmeter. The voltage shall be held at that value for 1 min.

E.8
Where it is more convenient to do so, the dielectric strength test may be made by applying a dc voltage instead of an ac voltage, provided that the voltage used is 1.4 times the values specified in Clause E.1.
